data.title: Gold Is A Safe Haven From Trumponomics
data.description:
Global financial markets have been rocked by the introduction of Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs, causing widespread disruption and uncertainty. As these tariffs have tanked $2.4 trillion in market value, investors scrambled to protect their assets, leading to a profit-taking gold sell-off. But gold is still the ultimate safe haven asset even as it sees a temporary tumble from investors cutting their losses, as evidenced by its new all-time highs.

With markets panicking, Trump has backtracked on his tariff plan to a flat 10% tariff across the board, with a pause on reciprocal tariffs for all but China. Gold has held strong, only briefly dipping below $3,000. And as markets overreact (and react irrationally) to major news, economic realities eventually have their way. Gold, silver, and platinum remain exempt from the tariffs, positioning precious metals to weather the trade war storm as gold retakes all-time highs and beyond.

Investors reassessed positions in light of the tariff-induced volatility, with some taking profits from gold’s recent surge to new all-time highs. This profit-taking was a natural response to the uncertainty caused by the tariffs, which have targeted a range of goods and sparked retaliatory measures, particularly from China. But as the tariff back-and-forth continues, gold keeps its shine as a store of value during turbulence and uncertainty.

More of the shock of the tariffs has now worn off and profit-taking is all but finished for this round of Trumpenomics, causing gold to rocket upward again beyond its previous pre-trade war heights. Gold won’t stop being the go-to investment during times of policy uncertainty, as central banks buy gold and individual investors stack their own to safeguard their portfolios against unpredictable shocks. In spite of temporary panicked sell-offs, that hasn’t changed, and never will.

Major indices puked in the wake of Trump’s tariff announcement, bouncing back up when the concession was announced. But from bust to bounce in equities, gold’s role as a non-correlated asset becomes even more obvious. Unlike stocks or bonds, which are directly tied to economic performance and policy decisions, gold’s value as an independent global store of value makes it a multi-thousand year refuge for those looking to mitigate risk. Even as the gold profit-takers sold, the yellow metal held up much better than most of the market.

Gold vs. USD, 1-Month

If the Fed decides that QE is needed to stave off a recession, gold will rocket even higher as inflationary money printing devalues the dollar. Among massive spending and a black hole deficit, all roads lead to money printing. Gold provides a safe haven, but also a measuring stick. Forget about comparing the dollar to other manipulated fiat currencies like the Euro and Yen; gold shows the true value of the dollar and just how far it falls relative to real money. QE will make gold rocket upward to awe-inspiring highs.

The sell-off after Trump’s tariff announcement was a reflection of market-wide panic and profit-taking, not a fundamental shift in gold’s value proposition. With concessions announced and some degree of the initial chaos subsiding, investors reassess their strategies and come back to the same clear-headed conclusion: gold is the one true safe haven from tariffs, money printing, and other forms of economic and political chaos.

Exempt from tariffs either way, gold’s new all-time high is only a preview of what’s likely to come later this year. We have the setup for a 2025 financial crisis taking shape that could cripple the US economy, gold is the only lifeboat.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boeing: Up, Up & Away!
data.description:
On our previous coverage of The Boeing Company (NYSE:BA) (NEOE:BA:CA) (BA.PR.A) we moved to a Sell rating on account of large headwinds from poor fundamentals and atrocious valuations.

We don't think the earnings estimates for 2025 will materialize. There is further dilution down the line as the mandatory convertible preferred shares get converted in 2027. A credit downgrade to junk or another very large equity issuance lies right ahead for the bulls in late 2025. The stock is likely to visit its lows near $140 in our view.

Source: Fasten Your Seatbelts

The stock comfortably exceeded that downside target and it did not even take all of April for it to get there.

We go over how the fundamentals are shaping up and why we maintain a Sell on this, despite it having exceeded our pessimistic expectations.

The Earnings Fantasy

We have followed earnings trajectories on a ton of companies over multiple years. Nowhere have we seen the collective analyst community get it so wrong for so long as we have on Boeing.

It's Deja Vu all over again."

Source: Yogi Berra

It has been three months since we last wrote on this and just have a look at the decline in estimates. These were the numbers three months back. Note the big drop over the preceding 6 months for every fiscal year. 2024 appears to have increased, but that is the huge increase in loss expectations.

Let's fast-forward to today. 2024 has gone, but estimates for every other year have been bludgeoned. As we expected, 2025 is shaping up to be yet another loss year.

But the numbers are still extremely optimistic. The earnings (or rather the losses) are based on revenues increasing by almost 30% in 2025 and another 15% in 2026.

This is a wild fantasy in today's environment. Tariffs have been a game changer for most industries and the airlines are at the epicenter of it. Canadians, for one, are opting to stay home and telling the US policies exactly what they think about them.

Of course, while Canada makes up a good chunk of travel to the US, it is not all of it. So, this might be okay if Canada was an outlier. It isn't.

Airlines have started cutting back on routes and some more are being weighed.

Delta Air Lines Inc. (DAL) did a solid downgrade of its guidance for 2025. In fact, DAL beat Boeing for the number of analyst downgrades in the last 3 months, with all 21 analysts being offside.

So, the way this will work is that airlines will slowly realize that even their early March downgrades are not capturing a fraction of this hurricane. Revenues are going to come in way below estimates. Earnings, will drop faster than revenues. Airlines are about the most leveraged operating model you can find and we would not be surprised to see 2025 earnings come 40%-60% below where we stand today. That, of course, means zero free cash flow as these are very high-capex industries. So, look for CEOs to start to cut back on capex plans, and some of this will happen even without them trying. After all, if planes fly less, fewer planes will need to be bought. Boeing will be the proud recipient of all of this angst, and those growth-promising revenue estimates will fold like a cheap suit in 3–6 months.

Verdict

We expect another banner year of losses for Boeing. In case anyone is tracking this, Boeing has gone through one entire economic cycle with large cumulative losses. That is a fairly rare feat for a cyclical stock that is supposed to leverage on the upside. So, the company is one of the poorest ways to play an expansion, and heaven knows you don't want to be caught holding this in a downturn. There are other factors weighing as well. None of the rating agencies are factoring in a recession, though they likely will be at the end of Q2-2025. The writing will be on the wall for Boeing to transition into “junk” land fairly soon. With a higher cost of capital and no profits in sight, this only has one hope to be salvaged. That hope is that value buyers' step in because it is cheap. Unfortunately, every value buyer who has done their homework knows this. Boeing is still incredibly expensive.

So expensive, that the equity could fall another 75% from here and not reach trough valuations seen at previous bottoms. The expensive part has come from the accumulation of a massive debt load and that means that EV to sales (not market cap to sales) is the only metric showing the real story.

So, this was the chart we were referring to in the title. The massive debt load renders any long-term positive outcome rather improbable. The stock might bounce a bit from its oversold state, but we expect $100 to be decisively taken out on the downside in 2025.

The Boeing Company DEP CONV PFD A (NYSE:BA.PR.A)

Some investors try and hide out in secondary securities, thinking that this protects them. In the case of BA.PR.A, it definitely does not. This is a mandatory convertible offering and offers no extra value over the common shares.

The conversion settlement rate will be 0.2914 shares per unit if the then current market price is greater than $171.5854 and 0.3497 shares per unit if the market price is equal to or less than $142.9797. For market prices between those values, the settlement rate will be $50 divided by the market value. The last reported sale price of the common stock on 10/28/2024 was $150.69 per share. The depositary shares are convertible in lots of 20 at any time at the holder’s option into 0.2914 shares of common stock per depositary share.

Source: Quantum Online

Yes, it does have a large dividend and that is more than what Boeing common shares offer. But that dividend is built into the higher price and if you work out total returns in different scenarios, the BA.PR.A shares will track the common shares of Boeing.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Commodities In Q1 2025: The DBC ETF Outperforms
data.description:
A diversified commodity index of 29 commodities trading in the futures market on U.S. and U.K. exchanges, including energy, base metals, precious metals, grains, soft commodities, and animal proteins, rose 3.44% in Q1 2025 after posting a 12.5% gain in 2024.

Commodities are global assets that reflect inflationary pressures and are highly sensitive to trade protocols. In Q1, the prospects of U.S. tariffs on worldwide trading partners caused increased volatility in many markets, pushing gold and copper to new record highs and silver to a multi-year peak. Other factors caused Arabica coffee and cattle futures to rise to the highest price in history over the first three months of 2025.

As the markets moved into Q2, President Trump’s April 2 “Liberation Day” ignited extreme volatility across all asset classes. A dramatic change in global trade protocols, ongoing wars in the Middle East and Ukraine, and the bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers have caused uncertainty and increased price variance in markets.

Commodities rallied in Q1

Five of the six commodity sectors posted gains over the first three months of 2025:

  • The grain and oilseed sector posted a marginal 0.05% gain in Q1.
  • Energy commodities moved 4.62% higher in Q1.
  • Base metals were up 6.45% during 2025’s first quarter.
  • Animal proteins rose 8.42% over the period.
  • Soft commodities, which led the commodities sector in 2023 and 2024, retreated, posting the only loss of 13.79%.
  • Precious metals were bullish beats, leading the commodities asset class with a 14.88% Q1 gain.

The U.S. dollar index (DXY) fell 4.07% in Q1, and U.S. thirty-year Treasury bond futures (US30Y) moved 3.32% higher over the period. A weaker dollar and lower interest rates traditionally support higher raw material prices.

The winners outnumbered the losers

Of forty commodities or commodity-related trading markets, twenty-four posted Q1 gains while sixteen moved lower.

The Q1 winners include:

The commodities that declined in Q1 include:

Only two double-digit percentage declines occurred. Frozen concentrated orange juice (JO1:COM) and cocoa (CC1:COM) futures both made new record highs in parabolic moves in 2024.

There were fourteen double-digit percentage gains, outnumbering the double-digit losers by 7:1.

The leaders- new highs in gold, COMEX copper, coffee, and cattle futures

Gold’s (XAUUSD:CUR) rally began in 1999 at $252.50 per ounce. Gold eclipsed the 1980 $875 all-time nominal high in 2008 and has not traded below $1,000 since 2009. After correcting from over $1,920 in 2011 to a $1,045.40 low in 2015, gold has made higher lows and higher highs over the past decade.

The quarterly chart shows that gold’s bullish path turned parabolic in 2023 and 2024, rising to a record $3,162 high in Q1. Gold was 18.24% higher in Q1, settling at $3,122.80 per ounce on March 31, 2025. Gold has made another new high at $3,263 in early Q2 as the bullish price action continues.

COMEX copper futures (HG1:COM) rallied to a new record $5.3740 high in Q4, posting a 25.02% quarterly gain. Nearby COMEX copper futures settled Q1 at $5.0340 per pound and dropped like a stone to a $4.03 low in early Q2 2025. Meanwhile, LME copper forwards lagged the futures, rising only 10.74% in Q1 because of tariff prospects.

Copper and silver (XAGUSD:CUR), which rose 18.36% in Q1, experienced the same tariff-related volatility as metal moved from Europe and other locations to the U.S. as the market prepared for the President’s April 2 “Liberation Day.” When copper and “bullion,” relating to precious metals, were not included in the tariffs, copper and silver prices imploded. However, copper rallied from the lows in volatile trading but remains below the Q1 closing price on April 15.

Arabica coffee futures (KC1:COM) on the Intercontinental Exchange reached a new record high in Q1.

The quarterly chart highlights the 18.76% Q1 rally in coffee futures. Coffee reached a record $4.2995 high before settling at $3.7975 on March 31, 2025, due to supply concerns from Brazil, the world’s leading coffee bean producer. Coffee futures were slightly lower in mid-April, but at over $3.70 per pound, they remain higher than the previous 1977 high of $3.3750.

Live (LC1:COM) and feeder cattle (FC1:COM) futures posted 8.45% and 8.91% respective Q1 gains, reaching record highs during the first three months of 2025.

The laggards: Gravity hits two high-flying soft commodities

The cure for high commodity prices tends to be those high prices. Extreme rallies can send prices to levels where producers increase output, inventories begin to build, and consumers seek substitutes, creating price tops.

In Q1, the two leading declining commodities had reached record highs with parabolic rallies in 2024.

While FCOJ futures dropped 51.05% in Q1, they rose to a record $5.4315 high in Q4 2024. The settlement price on March 31, 2025, was $2.43550 per pound, above the previous 2016 all-time high of $2.2750. In early Q2, FCOJ prices declined below the 2016 high, reaching $2.0380, but were above the 2016 peak on April 15 at the $3 level. Adverse weather and crop disease have caused Brazilian and Floridian supply concerns.

The chart shows the Q1 32.31% plunge in cocoa prices, settling at $7,902 per ton. Cocoa reached a record peak of $12,931 in Q4 2024. The pre-2024 all-time high was in 1977 at $5,104 per ton. Cocoa futures remained above the pre-2024 high at the end of Q1 and were higher than the Q1 closing level on April 15, 2025 at just over the $8,000 per ton level.

While FCOJ and cocoa futures fell over 50% and more than 30%, respectively, in Q1, the prices corrected from lofty levels, with both still in record territory in early Q2 2025.

DBC outperforms the commodities composite and the energy sector in Q1- Volatility in early Q2

The top holdings of the Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DBC) are currently:

The DBC ETF has a nearly 54% exposure to energy commodities. The diversified commodity ETF is energy-centric, but has double-digit percentage allocations to precious metals, base metals, and agricultural commodities.

The monthly chart shows that DBC rose 5.24%, moving from $21.38 at the end of 2024 to $22.50 per share on March 31, 2025. In Q1, DBC slightly outperformed the energy sector and the commodity composite.

The risk-off price action in April caused DBC to decline to the $20.89 per share level. DBC has $1.25 billion in assets under management, trades an average of over 2.35 million shares daily, and charges a 0.85% management fee.

DBC earns an A in liquidity from Seeking Alpha ETF Grades. The ETF paid end-of-year dividends over the past years, resulting in an A- grade. The high expense ratio earns a D in expenses. The price action receives a D in momentum, while the risk and volatility inherent in commodity trading earns a C+ in risk.

DBC outperformed energy and the commodities composite in Q1 and provides a diversified, yet energy-centric, approach for including commodity exposure for portfolios.

Demystify commodities in an era of uncertainty

Commodities experienced significant volatility in 2024, with energy prices impacted by geopolitical tensions, while precious metals and industrial metals saw significant gains. Looking ahead to 2025, the outlook remains murky, with trade policies shaping future commodity performance. During these times, you need someone who can share their knowledge and expertise to position for success.

The Hecht Commodity Report, led by seasoned Wall Street veteran Andrew Hecht, offers in-depth, actionable insights into everything from oil, gold, and grains to currencies and industrial metals. Subscribers receive weekly commentary, trade alerts, and forward-looking strategies and a proven approach that has helped investors navigate complex markets for decades, providing clear, actionable buy and sell recommendations.

Start your risk-free trial today and lock in the current price before it increases to $999 on April 30, 2025.

data.title: Even Without Tariffs, The Market Has Not Bottomed
data.description:
The past week has been quite the rollercoaster. Tariffs on and off again drove massive fluctuations and volatility in major stock indexes (such as the S&P 500 (SP500) or also reflected in SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY)).

While the market has narrowly focused on the tariffs and potential recession etc., I think the broader picture is that such an environment is not really good for the stock market and individual investors should not buy the dip too aggressively or with too high expectations, here’s why:

Valuation multiples are still at record highs…

As shown below (source), if we look at the Shiller PE ratio (based on average inflation-adjusted earnings from the previous 10 years, known as the Cyclically Adjusted PE Ratio developed by Robert Shiller) this indicator is still at 33, not much lower than record levels.

…but this is assuming a “perfect” environment, not high levels of policy uncertainty

A 30-40 valuation multiple for the overall market, substantially higher than the historical average of 17 in the past 50 years (as shown in the above chart), in my view is based on a “perfect” environment which is not what we are seeing now.

Take Apple Inc. (AAPL) as an example. Faced with tariff uncertainty, what is AAPL supposed to do? Move all of its supply chain from China to Vietnam? Or India? Or Mexico? Or move its supply chain 100% back to the US and produce/assemble each little screw domestically and take the hit to P&L? Any of these actions will cost billions of dollars if not much more and many years to implement. Even though it appears smartphones are exempt from reciprocal tariffs, for now, a new policy swerve could happen any day.

If policymakers keep changing their minds every year or 4 years, it will be incredibly difficult for companies to make these multi-year plans and investments. Let’s visualize this in terms of numbers:

  • AAPL says millions work in their supply chains. Let’s say the US moves back all of that 100% and with investments in robotics etc., 1 million US workers can produce AAPL products for the global market. Let’s say the employee compensation per head is $60,000 (which is the median income of full-time year-round employees and not particularly high), this would be $60 billion annually.
  • This $60 billion incremental labor costs would pretty much directly drop to the bottom line - the same worker in China earns about $3/hour (at 250 working days a year for 8 hours a day is $6,000 a year, about a tenth of what the hypothetical US worker would earn). $60 billion would be nearly half of AAPL’s trailing twelve months (TTM) earnings before tax ($125 billion).
  • This thought experiment shows to what extent major US companies like AAPL are reliant on “globalization” to achieve their current level of profitability and this higher level of profitability has also fueled their high valuation multiples: AAPL earned $96 billion net income TTM and has a PE of 30 for $2.86 trillion market cap. If AAPL directly or indirectly hired a million Americans for every little screw it might just earn $48 billion (but if the PE falls back to a more historical level of 15 due to lower profit margins etc.), the market cap would only be $720 billion.

Whether AAPL makes its products in some distant low-cost country or in the US is a policy decision up to the American people and leadership, but the spectrum of results could have vastly different impacts on AAPL’s financial results and if the policy goal shifts every year or few years, it becomes harder to justify a high valuation multiple.

While the market has been soothed temporarily with the 90-day pause on tariffs, in my view, the Pandora’s box has been opened and the myriad of uncertainties will continue to fester and multiply. For example, Europe has offered to reduce tariffs to 0 in exchange for 0 tariffs from the US, but also mentioned the possibility that they may retaliate against US big tech companies. But will the US accept a zero-tariff trade deal with Europe? Or what if a zero tariff trade deal with Europe is preliminarily accepted and in the next year or two, Europe still runs a large trade surplus with the US, would this cause the deal to be renegotiated? After the relatively predictable economic framework existing since the end of the Cold War is replaced with uncertainty as a negotiation strategy, then anything becomes possible. At some point, Europeans may very well decide it is difficult to figure out any long-term deal with the Americans and just make their economy more insular and inward-looking. I, personally, view Europeans developing local alternatives to US Big Tech companies as a significant possibility and risk to overall US stock prices, as I’ve previously discussed here.

The late 1970s/early 1980s generational buying opportunity was at low, not high valuations

There are some analysts who are saying to buy the dip. While that has worked for the past decade or so and may very well work this time, I would caution against viewing a 10% dip as a “generational buying opportunity”. In fact, the last time there was a generational buying opportunity, during the depths of the 2008 financial crisis, the cyclical adjusted PE reached a low of 13 in March 2009. Currently, despite a volatile few weeks, we are still at 33, substantially higher than even the peak period in 2006-2007 before the financial crisis; hence I believe we are nowhere near any “generational buying opportunity”.

Buffett’s sale now seems remarkably prescient

Buffett amassed a large amount of cash by selling down his stock portfolio last year. Many market commentators downplayed Buffett’s sales, but it seems like Buffett is once again prescient. Per Buffett’s past modus operandi, he typically isn’t buying and selling for 10% gains or losses. If Buffett is selling and stockpiling over $300 billion in cash, it’s probably because he sees valuations at unsustainably high levels and the time to buy will be when he is ploughing this dry powder back into the market.

Risks to bearish thesis

As an adage says, bear markets climb the wall of worry. US equities have outperformed many asset classes over the past decade, and this tariff uncertainty could be a short-term blip before things go back to normal. I would definitely not go short, though I would also not be too optimistic about the risks.

Conclusion

I would advise against any form of leverage for individual investors, either long or short. While equities have always regained their ground after a few decades (even if just due to inflation), short-term volatility magnified by leverage can saddle individual investors with large, irreparable losses. In fact, as I previously discussed, individual investor interest in leveraged products is probably a bearish, not a bullish sign.

For investors with a super long-time horizon (e.g., 30 years or 50 years), it may make sense to hold for the long run (timing the market is very difficult for the individual investor to pull off), though I would caution against any overly optimistic expectations of future returns.

For investors with shorter time horizons (e.g., need to use large amounts of money in the next few years), I would caution against sinking massive amounts of new money into stocks.

data.title: Verizon Q1 Preview: Defensive Play With A Solid Dividend, But Don't Rush The Entry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ) is a steady, dividend-focused investment that tends to perform well in times of market volatility. In the past months, while the S&P 500 (SP500) returned negative four percent, Verizon returned almost two percent in profit.

Verizon fits perfectly into the "defensive" category - a company that sells something people don't cut from their budget even when times get really tough. Most of us can't function without mobile service or TV, which makes Verizon's recurring revenue streams incredibly strong during downturns. At the same time, the stock is trading at a discount, pays a high yield, and is showing some real results in broadband and wireless segments of its business. Still, this upcoming report will be an important moment. If results don't meet expectations, and they might miss, there is a solid chance we will be able to see more attractive points in the days that follow.

Regardless, my rating for Verizon is Buy for those looking for a defensive dividend play and realizing that this isn't about the growth. If you are here for the growth or momentum, go ahead and skip this one out.

Company Overview

Verizon Communications is one of the three telecom giants dominating the U.S. market, with a heavy focus on postpaid mobile services, broadband expansion (high-speed Internet through new tech like fixed wireless access (FWA and fiber), and enterprise wireless infrastructure. In the most recent quarter, Q4 2024, the company delivered a performance that I would call steady but not showy. Normalized EPS came in at $1.10, which matched analyst expectations, while GAAP EPS beat consensus by $0.13. The company reported a revenue of $35.68 billion, also beating expectations by about $355 million. So, although the quarter wasn't an overwhelming surprise, the company did manage to outperform, which is always great.

More importantly, Verizon met or exceeded every major target it had set for 2024. Wireless service revenue grew by 3.1% year-over-year, which is right at the top end of the 2% to 3.5% guidance range. It's a very solid performance for a company of this scale. Adjusted EBITDA grew 2.1%, also well within the range, and full-year adjusted EPS came in at $4.71, slightly beating the high end of management's $4.70 target. Capital expenditures were kept under control at $17.1 billion, and free cash flow came in strong at $19.8 billion, well above the company's prior forecast of up to $18.5 billion. I've gathered the guidance for 2024 and the actual results in a table below:

Operationally, Verizon kept things moving in the right direction. It added nearly 1.6 million broadband subscribers over the year, mostly thanks to strong growth in fixed wireless access, which is wireless home internet they provide. Prepaid also turned positive for the first time since Verizon bought TracFone (TracFone is a U.S. prepaid wireless service that Verizon bought in 2021), which shows they're finally seeing some payoff there. On the financial side, the company kept reducing its debt, bringing the ratio of net unsecured debt to adjusted EBITDA down to 2.3x. That's a real improvement compared to last year and shows they're serious about getting their balance sheet in better shape. This is, by the way, something to follow in the future.

Earnings Expectations for Q1 2025

Verizon is scheduled to report its Q1 2025 earnings on April 22 before the market opens. Wall Street is forecasting normalized earnings per share of $1.15 and GAAP EPS of $1.13. Revenue is expected to come in at just over $33.26 billion.

The problem is that over the past three months, there have been more downward revisions than upward ones - seven analysts have lowered their EPS and revenue forecasts, while four have raised them for EPS and only two for the revenue. That tells me the mood around this report is pretty cautious, and if the numbers miss even a little, the market might not take it well.

Even though the majority of analysts' revisions for the next earnings are negative, just a few days ago, Evercore ISI upgraded the stock to "Outperform" and named it its top pick in the wireless sector. They noted that Verizon has meaningfully outperformed the broader market this year, even though it is still behind peers like T-Mobile and AT&T. According to Evercore, the market is underestimating Verizon's broadband strategy, especially its fiber and fixed wireless rollout. They also highlighted improving postpaid trends, solid business customer performance, and consistent delivery on subscriber targets. The firm raised their price target to $48, implying about 12% upside from current levels.

Notwithstanding some potential subscriber volatility, we remain confident in its [Verizon's] ability to meet its wireless service revenue growth target, Evercore said Friday.

Valuation

Verizon is currently trading at a pretty significant discount compared to the broader communication services sector. Its forward P/E is around 9.6, which is about 43% lower than the sector median. For a company that's delivering steady results and meeting its targets, that's a huge gap.

Important to note that I am referring to those looking for income and stability, and not for growth because if we look at the forward PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth), the stock is almost 350% overvalued. This is the reason why, at the beginning of this article, I suggested to growth-seekers to skip this one out.

The Quant score on Seeking Alpha gives Verizon a "B" for valuation, which feels justified. It looks like the stock is cheap because the market has been slow to recognize its turnaround efforts. It may not get re-rated dramatically unless growth accelerates, but with a solid free cash flow profile, Verizon doesn't need rapid growth to deliver shareholder returns, especially with the amazing dividend yield that it has.

Dividend

Let's be honest, Verizon's dividend is the main reason why investors hold the stock, and it's not hard to see why. The company returns an impressive 6.1% dividend yield, which makes it one of the highest-yielding large-cap names in the U.S. market. The company has increased its dividend for 20 years straight, which is great. The current payout ratio is around 58%, which is more than sustainable, particularly given the nearly $20 billion in free cash flow generated last year.

That said, Verizon's dividend safety rating currently sits at a "C+", and while it's not flashing red, it does suggest some pressure. Like most telecom giants, Verizon carries a lot of debt, it's part of the business. They build out massive infrastructure like 5G towers, fiber networks, etc., and then slowly cover it from the subscription fees of their clients. The bigger question is whether they can keep it under control, especially with slower earnings growth ahead, which could limit their ability to grow the dividend by much. But for yield-focused investors, it's not such a huge deal. The yield is still high, the payout is covered by cash flow, and the track record is consistent, so I would say it's a great dividend stock.

What Matters Most in This Report

There are a few things I'll be paying close attention to in this report. The first one is postpaid phone net additions - this is always a big deal for telecoms, and if Verizon keeps adding subscribers or at least doesn't lose any, it'll be a good sign. The second thing is broadband growth, especially in fixed wireless access, which has been their biggest success story over the past few quarters. I'll also be watching ARPU trends (average revenue per user) to see if they're managing to get more revenue per account without losing customers, which can always bring some additional gains to the stock price.

Final Thoughts

Verizon is not here to blow anyone away with growth, and that's okay. The company is doing what it needs to do - delivering stable results, protecting its margin, paying down debt, and returning money to shareholders. It may not be exciting, but in a market like this, boring can actually be a good thing.

If the company beats or stays in line with expectations, I think it'll keep moving higher, especially as more investors start investing in defensive plays with strong dividends. If it misses and the stock pulls back, it might open up a better entry point for long-term, income-focused investors.

My rating is Buy, but only if you're expecting consistency and stable cash flow from this company, and not fast growth. In fact, there almost isn't any growth at all, keep it in mind. If you are looking for a defensive play to hold through these uncertain times, Verizon might be a perfect option for you.

data.title: Advantage Energy: Q1 In View
data.description:
Advantage Energy Ltd. (OTCPK:AAVVF) recently updated shareholders with a new corporate presentation. This is a financially strong company that recently leveraged up to hasten its production mix change towards more liquids. But the natural gas exposure has likely made for a darn good first quarter that was not expected by the market. The stock price has been depressed probably because of the recent political uncertainty. But this well-managed company should come through the situation in better shape than many competitors. Eventually, that uncertainty should be replaced by something constructive, well-communicated, and permanent.

Last Article And Continuing Charlie Lake Update

My last article reported that the Charlie Lake acquisition was exceeding expectations. Now the company has some numbers to back up those claims.

The leverage here is high by company standards. However, it is also well within the debt market allowed ranges. The big deal here is to keep Mr. Market from being a bundle of nerves (or worse) until the deleveraging process is well underway.

Rumors are a way for Mr. Market to reduce uncertainty. The way to combat rumors is by doing exactly what this company is doing with frequent updates to keep rumors from ever getting started. The company will likely return to the conservative debt structure it is known for. However, market concerns about "getting there" need to be kept in check.

For this formerly dry gas producer that is slowly transitioning to more liquids production, that transition just accelerated. Readers may remember that last summer, natural gas pricing was so low, that drilling for liquids was one way to keep cash flow adequate.

Winter was unusually cold for many parts of North America, which gave natural gas prices a badly needed boost. Now the industry is a bit anxious as to what the rest of the year holds for the industry.

Generally, liquids are much more profitable for the company than just dry gas. This can be especially true outside the important winter heating season. Even during a period of weakening oil prices, the numbers above suggest a very profitable situation.

Every single well came in above budget with "one very large well." Obviously, more wells need to be drilled. But many other operators I follow have reported some darn good profitability from this interval. This company seems about to join that crowd.

As a result, the acquisition is now responsible for an outsized share of the operating income and cash flow. This shows what a little bit of the right liquids (outperformance) can do for a formerly dry gas producer.

The AFF that is being delivered (as shown above) likely means that the company will be in better shape than expected during the deleveraging process. Higher AFF likely means the EBITDA in the leverage calculation for the debt market and the bank will grow more than expected. Combine that with debt repayments and the whole thing will go faster than expected.

The income from this acquisition is probably going to grow in importance as time goes on.

Portfolio Profitability

The notes from above are very likely to change the profitability comparison shown below if those results are sustained for several years.

Keep in mind that Advantage Energy only has about 12 months (at the very most) of history of that Charlie Lake acreage. If the above-average results shown before continue, it will likely affect the comparison shown above in a material way.

Over Spring Breakup, the results shown above will be used to plan the fall and winter drilling campaign schedule. Charlie Lake acreage had the priority due to the fact that natural gas prices (NG1:COM) were so weak and the extent of that weakness duration was unknown. Over Spring Breakup, management will get a better view of natural gas prices to decide if more than acreage maintenance drilling will happen on the dry gas acreage.

This past summer, natural gas prices were nothing close to the chart assumptions. But the winter pricing changed that in a big way as natural gas prices eventually rose above the assumptions for oil prices.

However, even with that happening, oil and at least some liquids are generally more profitable than natural gas. It so happens that Advantage has some extremely profitable dry gas acreage that can make money with some very low prices. However, the acquisition results strongly imply that the company can adjust to a greater variety of industry conditions.

What is interesting to speculate is if Charlie Lake can replace Glacier as the most profitable part of the company over time.

Cost Comparison Caution

The company has long touted industry-leading costs. But costs for dry gas companies are naturally low on a BOE comparison basis. The real key is the profitability of the company, and the cash flow generated from those low costs.

By most accounts this has long been one of the more profitable companies in Canada despite a long history of dry gas production. The location of the dry gas leases turns out to have unusually low costs that manage to compete for capital with liquids-rich areas that the company also produces.

I still remember years ago when the company announced a "discovery" of liquids-rich production because technological advances made the interval competitive. But along came a technological innovation that nearly doubled the initial natural gas production from wells to make natural gas more profitable than the "discovery" at the time. It slowed the company's transition to more liquids.

Now the situation is reversed in that natural gas prices are very weak, so even the best acreage is not all that profitable for dry gas production. This winter changed that story with seasonally strong pricing during the winter heating season. But it remains to be seen how the rest of the year goes for the natural gas producers.

Summary

It was noted previously that the company "leveraged up" to buy the Lake Charlie acreage in the belief that the unusual profitability would be a material help to a company beset by low natural gas prices. It looks as though the results are proving to be a bit more than that.

This company could be in a very good position to report a great first quarter that benefitted not only from the acquisition but also from the strong natural gas prices. That is something that could make whatever comes next far easier to manage than is the case throughout much of the industry.

The debt ratio initially rose from the acquisition. But the results are good enough that the debt ratio could come down just from the unexpectedly good results from the drilling program.

In the last article, the company mentioned that it would drill solely Charlie Lake wells because dry gas prices were not good, and the future was a big wild card. The future is now a big wild card for very different reasons until the trade issues are resolved between the United States and Canada. However, the Charlie Lake wells may provide a cushion for this company to navigate whatever happens even though the debt is a bit higher than usual.

The continuing good results reinforce the strong buy recommendation. The company may well grow profitability as it grows production. Even if the economy becomes such that growing production is not a good idea, profitability could still increase during a period when prices are weak. That gives this low-cost producer an advantage over many competitors until commodity prices recover.

I would tend to hang on to this stock as long as the good news continues or until the stock gets way overpriced.

Risks

This article was written with the idea that tariff threats and trade war threats are bargaining positions that will be replaced with something constructive and permanent. There is no assurance that will happen.

The market action right now resembles an old-fashioned panic. Though I think this idea is a strong buy idea. I also do not stand in front of "runaway trains" like Mr. Market right now. However, I do think the current "throw them all out" will fade and be replaced by a situation where quality is important, and some logic will return. Old-fashioned panics never really lasted all that long.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company.

A loss of key personnel could materially set back the company's future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Banks Liking These Market Swings
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Bank earnings show strong trading revenue amid big swings. (0:16) HPE draws activist interest. (2:20) Fund managers have never been so worried about one outcome. (5:01)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, banks are making hay while the volatility sun shines, cashing in on a turbulent market.

Following a strong quarterly report Monday from Goldman Sachs (GS), thanks to equity sales and trading, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) and Citi (C) earnings reports exceeded Wall Street expectations, also showing trading outperformance.

BofA turned in a bottom-line and top-line beats, helped by sales and trading revenue growth at its Global Markets division.

CEO Brian Moynihan said the bank's year-over-year 18% increase in GAAP EPS "reflected growth in net interest income and fee income, while sales and trading delivered its 12th consecutive quarter of year-over-year revenue growth."

But he also said: “We potentially face a changing economy.”

Bank of America sees net interest income on fully taxable equivalent basis rising $15.5 billion-$15.7 billion by Q4 2025 (vs. Visible Alpha consensus of $15.6 billion). That would be up from $14.6 billion in Q1.

Citigroup reaffirmed 2025 guidance and its Q1 earnings and revenue beat estimates, with strong performance in its markets and investment banking businesses.

The bank kept its 2025 guidance unchanged, subject to macro and market conditions. It expects net interest income, ex-markets, up 2%-3% from 2024. Full-year revenue is expected to be ~$83.1 billion to $84.1 billion, compared with its prior view of $83.5 billion to $84.5 billion, vs. the average analyst estimate of $83.6 billion.

Ian Bezek, Investing Group Leader for Ian's Inside Corner, called the results excellent, with equity and fixed income trading results “particularly robust.” But he also noted a "15% rise in year-over-year costs related to potential credit losses, and that trend could accelerate in Q2 thanks to uncertainty from the tariff situation."

Among other active stocks, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) raised its full-year sales outlook with its Q1 results, while its sector is bracing for new tariffs under the Trump administration.

J&J reaffirmed its full-year EPS outlook of $10.50–$10.70 on a non-GAAP basis while raising its reported sales outlook to $91 billion to $91.8 billion from the $89.2 billion to $90 billion issued three months ago.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is gaining on a report that activist Elliott Investment Management has accumulated a more than $1.5 billion stake.

Bloomberg says Elliott is expected to engage with the software and networking company to help boost its share price, but specifics aren’t known.

And Albertsons (ACI) is struggling as the grocery chain’s soft EPS guidance offset a better-than-expected profit in the fourth quarter.

For 2025, the company is projecting adjusted EPS of $2.03 to $2.16, falling below the consensus estimate of $2.30 per. Additionally, identical sales are expected to increase 1.5% to 2.5% with adjusted EBITDA of $3.8 billion to $3.9 billion versus 2.0% growth in identical sales and adjusted EBITDA of $4.005 billion in the previous year.

In today’s trading, the major averages are bouncing around in choppy trading.

The S&P 500 (SP500) saw an ominous technical signal flash on Monday with the Death Cross. That’s when the 50-day moving average crosses below the 200-day moving average and, as its name implies, it’s bearish.

The last time that happened with the benchmark index was 3 years ago.

Meanwhile, Societe Generale noted that history has a warning for high-tariff environments.

Two of the biggest tariff eras in U.S. history saw a sharp decline in large-cap earnings.

Strategist Manish Kabra says: "The history of high tariffs shows extreme danger: 1890s McKinley Tariff led ~60% EPS drop in S&P 500; 1930s Smoot-Hawley Tariff led 70% drop."

Check out the chart.

Looking to the economy, the Empire State Manufacturing Index rose to -8.10 in April from -20 in March, compared with the -12.80 consensus.

Pantheon Macro economists Oliver Allen says: “Companies anticipate a sharp downturn ahead, with the expected business conditions index dropping to -7.4, from 12.7, the second-lowest reading since this component’s all-time low, in 2001. The drop in the capex expectations index, to 1.6, from 9.2, means it has now reversed essentially all of its initial jump in late 2024, consistent with a freeze on investment plans.”

“Meanwhile, the prices paid and received components jumped, both to their highest levels since September 2022, another indication of the building upward pressure on core goods inflation from the new tariffs,” he said.

In other news of note, Uber (UBER) and Waymo are readying for the launch of commercial robotaxi services in Atlanta.

Uber is now looking to see how many of its customers in the city are interested in a Waymo robotaxi service.

The two companies said in September 2024 that they were looking to launch in Atlanta and Austin in early 2025. Waymo services started on Uber in Austin in March.

Under the collaboration, Uber handles vehicle maintenance, charging and access management through its app. Waymo monitors the tech, autonomous operations and rider assistance.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA’s April Fund Manager Survey is out with a unique stat. Money managers have never been this worried about a single event.

A potential trade war and the threat of a resulting global recession is the biggest tail risk, with 80% of fund managers surveyed listing it as a concern. That’s the highest level of consensus around a single risk factor since BofA began tracking tail risks 15 years ago.

Beyond the looming threat of a trade war, other concerns, albeit to a far lesser extent, were inflationary pressures prompting aggressive interest rate hikes by the Federal Reserve and a potential crash in the U.S. dollar triggered by a foreign buyers’ strike. Worries about an AI bubble have almost disappeared, at 1%.

For fans of contrarian indicators, this is the 5th most bearish survey in 25 years, which may point to a time to buy.

data.title: Fiscal Flows, Bank Credit, And The Fed For April
data.description:
This article examines the US sectoral flows for March 2025 to assess their likely impact on markets as we advance into April 2025. It also includes an impact assessment of significant Fed and White House decisions and policies on risk asset markets. This is of particular importance as decisions are coming out of the Whitehouse at present that are causing sentiment events so severe that they override the liquid dynamics of the fiscal flows that would otherwise persist and create seasonal repeatable patterns with which to trade.

What happens in March affects what happens in April and often many time frames later. This is due to the lagged impact of fiscal flows on asset markets.

The table below shows the sectoral balances for the US, which are produced from the national accounts.

Source: US Treasury Department, FRED, and author calculations

March is seasonally a good month for fiscal flows, and this year has been an exception with weaker-than-normal federal deficit spending. The bad news is that April is also a bad month often associated with a private sector deficit caused by a federal government surplus budget. March saw a net addition of a solid $152 billion liquidity surplus in the private domestic sector.

Going into detail: The $152 billion private sector funds surplus came from a weaker-than-normal $165 billion injection of funds by the federal government (and this includes the new injection channel from the Fed of around $2.5 billion from interest on reserves that went directly into the banking sector).

The banking sector contributed a very moderate $88 billion. Banking credit has been relatively weak for the past two months. This may be because the debt limit impasse at the White House blocks the creation of treasury securities as collateral for lending and so crimps what the banks can offer within their capital asset ratios. Remember that banks are capital-constrained and not reserve-constrained.

The chart below shows how well private credit markets are performing.

Source: ANG Traders from the Away from the Herd SA investment group

The external sector extracted -$101 billion from the private domestic sector and deposited it into foreign-held bank accounts at the Fed (the external sector X) in return for imported goods and services.

The chart below shows the sectoral balance data plotted in nominal terms. The calculation is federal government spending or G plus the external sector (X and usually a negative factor). The result is how much money enters or leaves the private domestic sector or P, an accounting identity true by definition.

Source: US Treasury and SPX and author calculations

Last month, the green private domestic sector line made a weak movement upward, and this suggested the (SPX) would finish flat to slightly higher in the month, and the result was that the SPX finished 321 points lower. This large decline was associated with the flash crash sentiment event caused by the tariff statements coming out of the Whitehouse, discussed further below in this article.

This month going forward, the model is predicting the SPX should finish the month higher than it began. This is because of the strength of the fiscal flows. This is not a high bar to reach given the low finish mentioned above and the recent semi-reversal of the Trump Tariffs and the consequent V-shaped recovery of the stock market.

Any guidance from financial models such as the one above is extremely limited when there are sentiment events of such magnitude taking place. One thing that can be said is that the fiscal flows are strong enough to support a V-shaped recovery from such an event, and this is a good sign.

The table below shows the total federal government withdrawals from their accounts at the Federal Reserve Bank. A withdrawal by the federal government is a receipt/credit for the private sector and, therefore, a positive for asset markets.

Source: US Treasury Department, FRED, and author calculations

The table shows that total Federal government outlays made another decrease month over month and are now moving in the $2 trillion+ range. This month, we had a negative red flush in that all fiscal factors were negative. The federal deficit was much smaller than usual for March and points to weak risk asset markets going forward. A red flush is also present in the first table in this article concerning the fiscal flow acceleration and velocity effects and is also not a positive signal.

Federal government policy at present has declared the intent to 'tackle' the US's twin deficits. The federal spending deficit (which is the private sector's surplus) and the trade deficit.

The weaker federal withdrawals and federal deficit numbers show the federal government is spending less and reducing this deficit. The downside of this is that the private sector surplus is also reduced. Financial balances in the private sector are less.

Unlike the federal deficit, the trade deficit really is a financial deficit for the private DOMESTIC sector while at the same time providing a surplus in the form of real goods and services (A simple trade of money for things).

A trade deficit results in dollars leaving the private domestic sector (the US's domestic internal economy, and moving to bank accounts owned by foreigners). The bank accounts of US citizens and businesses go down, and the bank accounts of foreigners go up. Due to strict foreign ownership laws, only about 5% of these export earnings can re-enter the US domestic economy by way of stocks, bonds, or real estate; the rest are stored as Treasury securities and thereby lost to the domestic economy as is the coupon payment from it.

In the abstract, there is a two-sector model at work here. On the one hand, there is the US federal government, and on the other hand, the rest of the world is the private sector. The private sector can be further divided into a subgroup, the US domestic economy known as the private domestic sector and the rest of the world being the external sector.

Lower federal spending and trade tariffs lower the financial balances of the rest of the world and are recessionary.

One way of solving this effective impoundment of this money would be to relax foreign ownership laws so that the US dollar export earnings could be spent back into the US domestic economy. The other way is to stop trading, and another way is to tariff the export earnings out of existence.

If the current account deficit were balanced to zero, it would allow the sectoral balance in the US to improve as follows:

Private sector balance [P] = Federal government spending [G] + Net Exports [X]

For 2024:

P = 6.15% of GDP + (-3.9% of GDP) = 2.25% of GDP.

Let us assume the external sector is balanced for one reason or another.

P = 6.15% of GDP + 0% of GDP = 6.15% of GDP.

This looks great, and one might expect private domestic sector financial balances would almost triple now that all that federal government deficit spending is adding to private domestic sector bank accounts. The private domestic share moves from 2.25% to 6.16% now that 3.9% is not flowing to the external sector.

But there is a huge deficit of real goods and services, and this means shortages and higher prices as market demand bids them up. Domestic factories cannot be built overnight, and the $1.3 trillion dollars of goods and services that were formerly imported become a massive real shortage of goods and services. This means the extra 3.9% of GDP cannot be allocated to the private domestic sector, as it would create too much aggregate demand for goods and services that are not available to be bought.

In all likelihood, what would happen is Federal government expenditures would fall to 2.25% of GDP or, even worse, go to zero in a balanced budget scenario or to a minus scenario if the federal government was running the much-desired surplus budget.

That the elimination of the twin deficits is desirable is a fallacy of composition, as the mathematics shows. If it were achieved, the mathematical and physical result would be that financial balances in the private domestic sector would theoretically eventually run to zero in a surplus budget, and the pile of goods and services that were coming from overseas would no longer add to the US's real wealth.

The actual solution is that one should seek to import as many 9foreign goods and services as they are willing to exchange for treasury IOUs so that you have lots of material wealth AND the federal government should deficit spend to pay for the external sector deficit and also enough that the private domestic sector was operating at full capacity with full-employment and stable prices and the savings desires of citizens satisfied. Present Whitehouse policy is about as far away from this utopian situation as it is possible to be.

Fiscal milestones coming up: The bad news is that there is a large federal tax collection in the middle of April, where personal and corporate tax is paid. The tax drain will be moving in the $200 billion range. This seasonally puts a dent in the stock market one month later.

At the White House, the Trump tax cut legislation is bouncing back between the House and Senate and is currently on its second trip to the House. This back and forth could go on for a long time; however, they are aiming to have it done by Memorial Day.

In theory, the Republicans have a trifecta in that they have a majority in the House and Senate, control the office of the President, and could pass legislation quickly. However, internal divisions within the Republican Party prevent this from happening.

Until then, the debt limit is still in place and is creating a treasury drought. The chart below plots how this has affected markets in the past and how this one may play out.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

On the chart above, treasuries are shown as a dotted black line. On this chart, there are three debt limit periods shown, highlighted by the oblong boxes. The first dark black oblong box covers the 2021 debt limit, which was followed by the 2022 bear market in stocks [blue line]. This was coincidentally not just a protracted treasury drought but also a time of weak fiscal flows from the federal government and the banking sector. The stock market plunged with the treasury drought and stayed down in a bear market for about a year until fiscal flows improved.

The purple oblong box covers an area where there was a shorter but sharp treasury drought with a background of strong fiscal flows from federal government deficit spending and bank credit creation. There was a stock market plunge; however, it was not as deep as in 2022 and did not last as long. This was because the debt limit induced a treasury drought that took place against a background of strong fiscal flows.

The debt limit-induced treasury drought that we have now is shown in the area covered by the oblong box with the broken black lines. As for the 2021/22 debt limit period, this has been protracted, and the stock market has moved down hard for two reasons and is being led down by the falling operating cash balance [light pink) line and debt held by the public [black slashed line].

The first reason is the same as for the 2021/22 period: the fiscal flows are weak in terms of federal government deficit spending and bank credit creation; the last several months have seen decelerating fiscal flows. The second reason is sentiment. The new Trump government has moved so fast and so hard that it has unsettled market participants. So, the so-called "Liberation Day" on tariffs was a good example, as were the mass sackings and closures of many federal government departments.

Fear and Greed Index Today: 13 (Extreme Fear)

In my opinion, the Trump government appears to be led by hard-line Libertarians.

Libertarian: Anti-government advocates of deregulation to disable the public ability to tax and govern finance, real estate and other rent-seeking. The effect is to centralize planning in the hands of the financial sector – Wall Street and its satellites in the City of London, the International Monetary Fund [IMF] and the European Central Bank [ECB]. The aim of libertarian planning is privatization, leading to economic polarization, oligarchy, debt peonage and neofeudalism. What the libertarian (that is, financialization) argument leaves out of account is that taxing land rent and other unearned rentier income requires a strong enough government to rein in the vested interests. Opposing government has the effect of blocking such public power. Libertarianism thus serves as a handmaiden to oligarchy as opposed to democracy.

Hudson, Michael. J IS FOR JUNK ECONOMICS: A Guide To Reality In An Age Of Deception (p. 209). ISLET/Verlag. Kindle Edition.

As naive true believers, it is possible the administration will not raise or remove the debt limit just to see what happens. An attitude that I have heard more than once on this subject from government officials is along the lines of 'let's try it and see what happens'. The idea is to reduce the size of the federal government by defunding it.

The Liberation Day tariff debacle is an example of the naive, extreme economic approach taken. If this were applied to the debt limit, the result would be more serious than the tariffs debacle. This is because, at the macro level, the US external sector only accounts for about 4% of GDP, leaving the other 96% unaffected. Big headlines, big stock market drop but only a tiny portion of GDP and this is covered in this article in more detail.

As shown above, federal government deficit spending represents 6.15% of GDP and provides the treasury collateral that allows the banking sector to extend loans. If you cut off one, you cut off the other en passant. The federal budget impacts 100% of the US domestic economy and the markets located in it.

Even if the new budget with debt limit relief is passed, the proposed budget is so meager that a strong market rally cannot be expected from it. There will be a relief rally as the flush of blocked treasuries are issued, and this could be traded. However, the budget itself does not propose much in the way of big spending. I have reviewed the document, and in my opinion, it is a 'rob the poor and give to the rich' budget. It has large budget cuts for most forms of welfare that make fiscal space for a continuation of past tax cuts due to expire.

It is likely to continue the ongoing trend of ever weaker fiscal flows that have so far been the hallmark of the Trump presidency.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

The chart above is a zoomed version of the one before. Important to note is the thick blue line. This line is the change rate of debt issuance. One can see how the change rate of the SPX [orange line] follows the blue line, as does the change rate of Bitcoin (GBTC). As the chart notes above, "as the change rates rise, stock, and crypto markets rise as well… and vice versa."

All the lines are led by the change rate of Fed Aggregate Credit [light blue line], which is set to move all markets downward.

From a nominal first derivative perspective, the (SPX) dull green line cannot move upward and make new highs in an environment where the debt held by the public [purple line] does not rise. It is range-bound by the lack of liquidity and vulnerable to weakness when a bad sentiment event comes.

This situation gets worse when one looks at the big macro world picture at the end of this article.

The Federal Reserve concluded its March 2025 meeting, leaving unchanged its overnight borrowing rate in a range between 4.25%-4.5%.

The next meeting is at the end of this month, and it will be interesting to see what they do, given the new data and volatile times.

The following chart plots key indicators used to estimate what the Fed might do next, and has proven very predictive.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

The federal funds rate [FFR] is led by a multitude of co-factors. This month, all the co-factors are pointing to the Fed lowering the policy rate. From the top of the legend to the bottom:

1. The green GDP line is falling.

2. The pink change rate of the 10-year bond yield has fallen, as has the nominal purple line.

3. The black broken SPX line has fallen, as has the nominal orange line.

4. The brown unemployment line, which is inverted, has fallen, meaning more unemployment.

The economy is failing, and the Fed may well lower the FFR to address growth issues. All the data is there for a lowering of rates, and any decision not to will be political rather than technical. Often, central bank mandarins will seek to discipline federal governments by exposing them to bond yield movements they could otherwise have controlled. The British prime minister, Liz Truss, was ejected from office by the lack of action of her central banker, who could have at all times controlled the yield curve anywhere he chose to have it by the buying and selling of bonds on the central bank's market account. The bond market allegedly showed its displeasure at her new federal budget, and the Central Bank of England let it happen. This happened to Trump when bond yields ticked up several days into Liberation Day, as the chart below shows.

US 10-Year Treasury Bond Note Yield - Quote - Chart - Historical Data - News

On the chart above, the orange ovals show where the bond market disciplined Trump with the threat of more expensive national debt servicing in the future. The bond rate whipped up, suggesting that the Treasury must issue higher premium treasury securities in the future. This increases the Federal government deficit and national debt, which is seen incorrectly by the mainstream as bad. In mathematical fact the treasury interest adds to private sector income and the larger national debt increases the money supply and adds to financial sector balances after the following accounting identity correct by definition:

Federal Government Deficit = Private Sector Surplus

My opinion is that Congress needs to come together and take away Presidential discretion regarding the emergency use of tariffs (what emergency) and place this power firmly with Congress.

The Trump 1 presidency indicated this was a problem, though tariffs on China introduced under Trump (2018) were reviewed and ultimately maintained by the Biden administration and in some areas enlarged. But this was done incrementally, without the showman's shock and awe tactics being used now.

I believe the Trump 2 presidency shows how the tariff power lever can be abused. Congress had four years to close this loophole during the Biden presidency but did not do it, and now the bill has come in to be paid. Now that the hammer has fallen, there is renewed talk in Congress to pass such a bill.

“Trade wars can be devastating, which is why the Founding Fathers gave Congress the clear Constitutional authority over war and trade,” Cantwell wrote in a statement Thursday. “This bill reasserts Congress’s role over trade policy to ensure rules-based trade policies are transparent, consistent, and benefit the American public.”

Source: Senator Cantwell.

At the world macroeconomic level, we have the G5 and C5 chart below. The chart shows the spending level by the top five world governments (the G5) in a change rate format together with a range of risk assets such as gold (GLD), (DXY), and the stock market (SPX). The C5 are the balance sheets of the five largest central banks.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

Economic outcomes are preprogrammed from spending patterns that happen up to 18 months before. The brown and blue lines are fiscal carrier waves along which other smaller fiscal impulses resonate.

On the chart above the brown G5 line, expenditures of the world's largest federal governments lead asset markets such as (GBTC) and other crypto and the stock market (MSCI) by about 18 months, as does the blue line of the aggregate balance sheets of the largest central banks, the C5.

At this scale, this is a scary chart. The big strategic rollover of G5 and C5. Note the air pocket of G5 and Chinese credit impulse [green broken line] right about now. The stock market weakness was preprogrammed, and Trump made a gradual decline rout with his tariff statements.

The chart below is the same information as above but zoomed in with more details, down to a quarterly basis.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

If you have not been paying attention to this model of Global Liquidity, I believe it is essential to pay attention to it now.

The model says we have an inflection point higher in risk assets until May 2025 -- then the floor caves in. Bad times until August 2025. The cyclical downturn is NOT over yet. Q4 2025 will be a superb up-cycle for risk assets -- then the entire floor system gives way -- and no stopping until Q1 2027.

This model is always available, always live at the PAM Analytics (First Principles) channel 24x7.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group Forum chat room.

All of this needs to be seen from behind the lens of the law of rent that drives the land cycle on an even longer 20-year time frame.

Law of Rent

The law of rent states that land captures all the gains.

No matter what improvement is made to make the economy more productive and free more income for other things, it ends up flowing into the locational value of land. It is to economics what the law of gravity is to physics.

(Source: Mr Phil Anderson, Property Share Market Economics)

United States FHFA House Price Index

This is the chart to watch. The chart is for the US housing price index. The green circles highlight what is known in land cycle theory as a mid-cycle crisis.

The two green circles from left to right are more commonly known as the 2000 Dot Com crash and the 2020 COVID crash. Here, the stock market went down, but the land price did not. That is the key point. They were serious falls, but not threateningly systemic to the finance system.

The red circles highlight what is known in land cycle theory as land-led recessions. The important difference is that at the time shown circled green, the land price was unaffected and kept rising. In the areas shown circled in red, the land price rolled over and went down.

From left to right, the first red circle is the Global Financial Crisis [GFC] of 2006-09. The second red circle is GFC 2, which is still in development.

At the moment, the land price is still rising, so from a land cycle perspective, there is no problem. See the red arrows next to the red circles. In the current cycle, the arrow is still rising and at a steep gradient; it has not flattened, which is the first warning, and it is definitely not going down yet.

In 2006, the first red circle, the land price went down and did so for four years of falling property prices and this is what gave us the financial crisis because the banks lost so much equity as the asset backing for their loan liabilities lost value.

This is the accounting formula:

Equity = Assets - Liabilities.

Mortgages collateralized against properties are the largest part of the credit capital structure, and when property values fall, it brings down the mortgage market and creates a run on the banks, and you get a land-led recession with a serious financial crisis because liabilities exceed assets as the asset prices fall.

The land cycle peak and rollover are not here yet, but it is getting close, and now is the time to reduce real estate exposure and leverage.

The chart below shows how this cycle has been repeating since 1955.

The table below shows how this cycle has been repeating since 1902.

Source:

Akhil Patel. The Secret Wealth Advantage: How you can profit from the economy’s hidden cycle (p. 410). Harriman House. Kindle Edition.

Please like, share, and subscribe.

data.title: PepsiCo Q1 Preview: This Isn't The Time To Chase PEP's Dividend
data.description:
Investment Thesis

If you look at the dividend yield, PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) might seem like a perfect long-term buy. The stock suggests a 3.75% yield, 52 years of dividend growth, and consistent quarterly payouts. Sounds like exactly the kind of stock you’d want to hold through a downturn in the market, especially given the recession stays a strong possibility later this year. However, I believe this is one of those cases where you have to look deeper to fully understand what to expect from the stock before investing in it, particularly with earnings just around the corner.

Right now, I don’t think it’s the best time to chase PepsiCo’s dividend. If I were considering a defensive position, I’d rather look at other stocks in the sector that show better momentum or stronger fundamentals going into earnings.

Company Overview

PepsiCo is known globally for its beverage and snack portfolio, with a presence in nearly every grocery store. It’s long been viewed as a steady, defensive stock, especially popular with dividend investors, and not without reason. The company has a 52-year history of dividend growth, which is an insane amount of time compared to the 2 years for the sector on average.

PepsiCo’s Dividend

PepsiCo’s 3.75% dividend yield is about 25% higher than the sector median. It’s also well above its own five-year average of 2.9%. For dividend investors, that looks like a great entry point. But once you dig into the payout metrics, the picture changes slightly.

One thing that stood out to me is the Dividend Safety Grade of A-. At first sight, that looks reassuring, but once you break down the numbers, it doesn’t really add up. Most of the underlying metrics are actually weak: the dividend coverage ratio is rated D, total debt-to-equity sits at an alarming 262%, and the cash dividend payout ratio exceeds 100%.

The only reasons for an A- rating are backward-looking metrics like strong cash from operations and high return on equity. PepsiCo is profitable and generates a lot of cash, without a doubt, but when the payout structure is already stretched, it doesn’t take much of a slowdown to create pressure. It doesn't mean that something will happen to the company or even to the dividend. Usually, dividend giants like this have a few tricks up their sleeves to ensure some dividend growth, even if it’s minimal, but I wouldn’t call it a perfect situation either.

Valuation: Cheaper, But Not Cheap Enough

Even after a year of underperformance, PepsiCo is not trading at a bargain. PEP stock is down ~14% in one year, while S&P 500 (SP500) returned about 3%.

Its forward non-GAAP P/E stands at 17.46, roughly 12% above the sector median. The PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth) is very high: 3.69 compared to 2.34 for the sector. It means that the company doesn’t show the growth it was supposed to show, given the premium valuation.

It is true that PEP is trading ~25% below its 5-year average valuation multiples, including P/E and EV/Sales. But that discount reflects the market’s lower expectations. There’s no real margin of safety here unless you believe the company is about to surprise to the upside, which leads us to the next point, the upcoming earnings.

Wall Street Is Clearly Preparing for Weakness

In the past 3 months, analysts have issued 12 downward EPS revisions for the upcoming quarter and zero upward ones. The same is true for revenue: 8 downward revisions, zero upward.

This is definitely a signal that the company might show weaker than expected results. Factor grades on Seeking Alpha, by the way, confirm the trend: the Revisions grade dropped to a D, and the Growth grade is stuck at D+ with no improvement over the past 6 months.

The company is expected to report Q1 2025 earnings on April 24, pre-market. The current EPS estimate is $1.51 on $17.77 billion in revenue.

Underperformance Tells You All You Need to Know

Besides the future expectations and the possibility for earnings miss, the stock has been underperforming for a while now. PepsiCo (PEP) is down 14.2% over the past year, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 3.2%. Coca-Cola (KO) is up almost 21% in the same period. Even the Consumer Staples ETF (XLP), which is not exactly known for high returns, gained over 8%.

It means PepsiCo is seriously underperforming compared to its peers, and even a higher-than-average dividend yield doesn’t make up for this underperformance.

A few weeks ago, I wanted to buy KO stock out of the recession concerns, but after analyzing its performance throughout the recession, I realized it performs pretty much in line with the market during downturns, and it’s a solid play if you are in for the dividend. It wasn't what I was looking for, so I wrote an article stating that KO wouldn’t save you from the recession and rated KO stock as a sell. I noted that I wouldn’t exit if I were in for some time now, and my message was that even the biggest brand like Coca-Cola won’t protect you if the crash comes.

Regarding PepsiCo, I can say that the downside looks more serious than the upside. Yes, the fear in the market could serve as a possible catalyst for the stock price, but the performance of the company itself has a much stronger effect on the stock. The future doesn’t look that optimistic, and while the dividend yield without the question is really attractive, I am not ready to lose 10-15-20% of my money for less than a 4% yearly dividend yield.

Conclusion

PepsiCo is still a great company with iconic brands and a loyal consumer base. But right now, I don’t think the stock deserves the unquestioned confidence. The dividend looks nice on paper, but the safety metrics tell a different story. Valuation is still premium, growth doesn’t justify this valuation, and analysts are cutting estimates, which is a serious sign for the future underperformance of the stock price.

In a different environment, I might have looked at PEP as a stable dividend play. But today, with earnings risk on the table and clear underperformance versus both peers and the market, I think it's better to stay on the sidelines. I’m not saying the stock will collapse, but if it slips another 10% or more, I won’t be surprised. I will be more surprised if the stock price continues moving upward from now on. I rate PepsiCo as a Sell, and if I wanted exposure to defensive names, I’d be looking at some other tickers. For example, I would start with those consumer staples having the highest Quant Ratings.
